author: @paddy2661
text: That's cool so would the carbon handle 12v 500ma , is there a ohoms law for carbon,  thanks Robert cheers

replies.author: @seabeepirate
replies.text: Ohm’s law isn’t for a specific material. It works as well for carbon fiber as it does for copper. 12v and 500ma will still be 6 watts in both materials. Since you know the voltage and current you want to use, you can just measure the resistance of the carbon fiber and with that you can see how long or short to cut it.

author: @401ksolar
text: I have noticed that manufacturers who make semiconductor heat tape to keep water lines from freezing use a physical crimp where the cord goes in to contact with the semiconductor material, most of them use injection molding which leads to a dry connection, the ones that have a two-piece plastic cover tend to corrode and stop working after a year or two.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you can put a crimp over it as well mate but for that you need crimps and a crimping tool - tomorrow short lolol

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yes I like the idea of a physical connection and soldered and injection molded but that's difficult to do in a do-it-yourself setting LOL

author: @jp-wd9qy
text: What voltage can that thing you made take?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is really going to depend on what you stick it in - if used for heated clothing or pad heating - no worries - if you plan on a 3 phase kiln you might want to beef it up a bit with a crimp

replies.author: @fk-hi6gs
replies.text: Easy to find out on the web, just search carbon fiber thermal conductivity tables.
